back: A graph is planar if and only if it contains no subgraph that is a subdivision of K5 or K3,3.

front: What is K5 in graph theory?
back: K5 is the complete graph on 5 vertices where every pair of vertices is connected by a unique edge. It has 10 edges.

front: What is K3,3 in graph theory?
back: K3,3 is the complete bipartite graph with two sets of 3 vertices, where each vertex in one set connects to all vertices in the other set. It has 9 edges.
No, K5 is non-planar. It cannot be drawn on a plane without edge crossings.
No, K3,3 is non-planar. It cannot be drawn on a plane without edge crossings.
Edge subdivision replaces an edge (u,v) by introducing a new vertex w and two edges (u,w) and (w,v). It maintains the graph's structure while adding intermediate vertices.
Two graphs are homeomorphic if they can both be obtained from the same graph by a sequence of edge subdivisions.
A subdivision graph of G is formed by subdividing one or more edges of G. Also called a homeomorph of G.

front: Who proved Kuratowski's Theorem and when?
back: Kazimierz Kuratowski proved this theorem in 1930, providing a complete characterization of planar graphs.

front: What are the two fundamental non-planar graphs?
back: K5 (complete graph on 5 vertices) and K3,3 (complete bipartite graph with 3+3 vertices) are the two fundamental non-planar graphs.
A topological minor of G is a graph obtained by subdividing edges of a subgraph of G. It is closely related to Kuratowski's characterization.
It provides a complete and finite characterization of planar graphs, answering the fundamental question of when a graph can be drawn without crossings.

front: Can you add vertices to K3,3 to make it planar?
back: Yes, by subdividing edges of K3,3, you can create planar graphs. However, any subdivision of K3,3 itself remains non-planar.
A planar embedding is a drawing of a graph on a plane with no edge crossings. A graph is planar if such an embedding exists.
